out_of_scope: Prokaryotic vs eukaryotic comparisons beyond GCSE level
classification_hints: Question mentions cells, organelles, microscopes, mitosis, osmosis, diffusion, active transport or cell cultures.
common_misconceptions: Students often confuse osmosis with diffusion; they may also think cells 'use up' energy during diffusion.

common_misclassifications.reason: Tissues and organs are built from cells, but the question focus must be on cellular processes or structures to stay in 4.1.
Respiration and photosynthesis are cellular processes, but 4.4 focuses on the whole-organism bioenergetics context.

scope: Nucleus, cytoplasm, cell membrane, mitochondria, ribosomes
Plant cell wall, chloroplasts, permanent vacuole
classification_hints: Question asks to label or compare cell structures.
common_misconceptions: Students may think all cells have a cell wall.

common_misclassifications.reason: Both involve cell structure; 4.1.2 focuses on how structure relates to function in specialised cells.

scope: Differentiation as change in gene expression
Stem cells in animals and plants
out_of_scope: Embryonic stem cell ethics beyond GCSE scope
classification_hints: Question asks how a cell is adapted to its function or about stem cell potential.
common_misconceptions: Students may believe specialised cells lose their DNA.

common_misclassifications.reason: Tissue organisation also involves specialised cells; 4.1.2 focuses on the cell itself.

scope: Calculating magnification from scale bars or image size
out_of_scope: Staining protocols not required by the specification
classification_hints: Question involves microscope use, magnification calculation, or observing cells.
common_misconceptions: Students may confuse magnification with resolution.

common_misclassifications.reason: Both are practical skills; 4.1.4 focuses on culturing microorganisms.

scope: Calculating population size using serial dilution
Required practical: use of a light microscope
classification_hints: Question involves bacterial growth, colonies, aseptic technique or population calculations.
common_misconceptions: Students may think all bacteria are pathogens.

common_misclassifications.reason: Microorganisms are also discussed in infection; 4.1.4 focuses on growth and culture technique.

scope: Cell division in growth, repair and asexual reproduction
out_of_scope: Cancer as a separate topic (covered in 4.2)
classification_hints: Question asks about stages of the cell cycle, mitosis, or growth by cell division.
common_misconceptions: Students may confuse mitosis and meiosis.

common_misclassifications.reason: Reproduction involves cell division; 4.1.5 focuses on the process itself.

scope: Exchange surfaces and surface area to volume ratio
classification_hints: Question asks about movement of substances across membranes or exchange surface adaptations.
common_misconceptions: Students often think osmosis is only about water moving into cells.

common_misclassifications.reason: Gas exchange and absorption involve diffusion; 4.1.6 focuses on the mechanism at the cellular level.

classification_hints: Question concerns tissues, organs, organ systems, or whole-organism structure and function.
common_misconceptions: Students may confuse tissue with organ.

common_misclassifications.reason: Cells build tissues, but 4.2 focuses on organisation above the cell level.

scope: Examples of organisation in animals and plants
classification_hints: Question asks about levels of organisation or definitions of tissue/organ/system.
common_misconceptions: Students may include organelles as a level of organisation.

topics.topic_name: Animal tissues, organs and organ systems
out_of_scope: Surgical or pharmacological treatments beyond GCSE
classification_hints: Question asks about human organ systems, tissues, or lifestyle/disease links.
common_misconceptions: Students may think arteries carry oxygenated blood only.

common_misclassifications.reason: Disease is involved in both; 4.2.2 focuses on non-communicable disease and organ function.

topics.topic_name: Plant tissues, organs and organ systems
classification_hints: Question concerns plant structure, transport, or water movement.
common_misconceptions: Students may think phloem transports water.

common_misclassifications.reason: Water movement in plants relies on osmosis; 4.2.3 focuses on whole-plant transport systems.

scope: Viral, bacterial, fungal and protist diseases
out_of_scope: Detailed mechanisms of monoclonal antibody production
classification_hints: Question mentions pathogens, immune response, vaccination, antibiotics, or disease transmission.
common_misconceptions: Students may think antibiotics kill viruses.

common_misclassifications.reason: Disease is shared; 4.3 focuses on communicable disease and infection response.

scope: Human defence systems: skin, platelets, white blood cells
out_of_scope: Specific immune cell types beyond GCSE
classification_hints: Question asks how a disease spreads or how the body defends itself.
common_misconceptions: Students may think all white blood cells produce antibodies.

scope: Monoclonal antibodies: uses and basic principle
classification_hints: Question asks about vaccines, antibiotics, drug testing or monoclonal antibodies.
common_misconceptions: Students may think vaccines treat existing infections.

common_misclassifications.reason: Both involve disease; this topic focuses on prevention and treatment.

classification_hints: Question concerns plant pathogens, symptoms or defences.
common_misconceptions: Students may think plants do not have defences.

out_of_scope: Anaerobic respiration in microorganisms for biotechnology
classification_hints: Question involves photosynthesis, respiration, exercise or metabolic reactions.
common_misconceptions: Students may think plants only respire at night.

common_misclassifications.reason: Respiration and photosynthesis occur in cells; 4.4 treats them as organism-level processes.

scope: Required practical: effect of light intensity on photosynthesis
classification_hints: Question asks about photosynthesis, limiting factors or related practical.
common_misconceptions: Students may think plants get most of their mass from soil.

common_misclassifications.reason: Both are bioenergetics; 4.4.1 is about energy capture, 4.4.2 about energy release.

scope: Anaerobic respiration in animals and plants
classification_hints: Question asks about respiration, oxygen debt, exercise or metabolic rate.
common_misconceptions: Students may confuse breathing with respiration.

common_misclassifications.reason: Exercise response involves the circulatory and breathing systems; 4.4.2 focuses on energy release.

classification_hints: Question concerns control systems, receptors, effectors, hormones or nerve impulses.
common_misconceptions: Students may think hormones travel through nerves.

common_misclassifications.reason: The nervous and endocrine systems are organ systems; 4.5 focuses on control and coordination.

classification_hints: Question asks about maintaining internal environment or blood glucose regulation.
common_misconceptions: Students may think homeostasis keeps conditions absolutely constant.

common_misclassifications.reason: Blood glucose involves hormones; 4.5.1 is the overarching control concept.

classification_hints: Question involves neurons, reflex arcs, the eye or brain structure.
common_misconceptions: Students may think electrical impulses travel through hormones.

classification_hints: Question asks about hormones, menstrual cycle, contraception or plant growth substances.
common_misconceptions: Students may confuse FSH and LH roles in the menstrual cycle.

common_misclassifications.reason: Both are coordination systems; 4.5.3 uses hormones rather than nerve impulses.

classification_hints: Question involves inheritance, DNA, genes, alleles, natural selection, GM or classification.
common_misconceptions: Students may think acquired characteristics can be inherited.

common_misclassifications.reason: Cell division supports reproduction; 4.6 focuses on inheritance patterns.

classification_hints: Question asks about inheritance, Punnett squares, genetic crosses or sex determination.
common_misconceptions: Students may confuse dominant and recessive alleles.

common_misclassifications.reason: Variation and evolution build on inheritance; 4.6.1 focuses on reproductive and genetic mechanisms.

classification_hints: Question asks about natural selection, selective breeding, genetic modification or cloning.
common_misconceptions: Students may think evolution is directed toward a goal.

common_misclassifications.reason: Genetic modification is also technology; 4.6.2 focuses on variation and selection.

classification_hints: Question involves fossils, extinction, resistance or classification of organisms.
common_misconceptions: Students may think fossils are the only evidence for evolution.

common_misclassifications.reason: Ecology also studies organisms and environment; 4.6.3 focuses on evolutionary evidence and taxonomy.

classification_hints: Question mentions ecosystems, food chains, biodiversity, sampling, or farming impacts.
common_misconceptions: Students may think food chains begin with consumers.

common_misclassifications.reason: Both involve organisms and environment; 4.7 focuses on interactions and energy flow.

classification_hints: Question asks about adaptations or factors affecting organisms.
common_misconceptions: Students may think competition is only between different species.

scope: Required practical: field investigation of abundance/distribution
out_of_scope: Ecological pyramids of energy and numbers beyond GCSE
classification_hints: Question involves food chains, biomass pyramids or sampling methods.
common_misconceptions: Students may confuse biomass with population size.

common_misclassifications.reason: Both are ecology; 4.7.2 focuses on energy flow and feeding relationships.

topics.topic_name: Biodiversity and the effect of human interaction
classification_hints: Question asks about conservation, sustainability, or human impact on ecosystems.
common_misconceptions: Students may think sustainable farming means no farming.

common_misclassifications.reason: Human impact can alter food webs; 4.7.3 focuses on biodiversity and sustainability.

scope: Summary of key concepts that connect topics across biology
out_of_scope: New content not covered elsewhere in the specification
classification_hints: Question explicitly draws together ideas from multiple topics or asks for overarching biological principles.
common_misconceptions: Students may treat key ideas as a separate topic rather than a synoptic lens.

common_misclassifications.reason: Key ideas overlap with all topics; classify to the most specific topic unless the item is explicitly synoptic.
